scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the specific dispensation granted by King Philip V (the Macedonian ruler) to his Athenian captives during the Social War.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The event described by Plutarch occurred during Philip V's campaign against the Achaean and Aetolian leagues in 218 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the constitutional term limits (the inability to serve consecutive years) for the strategos (general) of the Achaean League.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This rule was in effect throughout Aratus's career; -245 marks his first year as general.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly refers to the disarmament of the residents of Argos by the tyrant Aristomachus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristomachus the Elder ruled Argos as a tyrant during the middle of the 3rd century BCE; -240 is used as a representative year of his reign.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to the Panhellenic convention of 'asylia' (asylum) granted to contestants traveling to the sacred Nemean or Isthmian games.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch notes Aratus's specific violation of this Greek-wide custom during his attack on Argos in -235.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the customary military law (ius belli) regarding booty and captives practiced by the Achaean and allied forces during the 3rd century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch describes these customs specifically in the context of the capture of Pellene in -241.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the enfranchisement of resident aliens specifically within the city of Mantineia after its capture.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aratus captured Mantineia and reorganized its citizenship structure in -226.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the power of the Achaean Federal Assembly over the financial resources of its elected generals.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The assembly at Aegium that stripped Aratus of funding followed the Battle of Mount Lycaeum in -227.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the enfranchisement of 'perioikoi' or resident aliens specifically as part of Cleomenes III's Spartan reforms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Cleomenes III carried out his revolution in Sparta, including these citizenship grants, in -227.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the abolition of existing property rights and the redistribution of land (ges anadasmos) in Sparta.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Cleomenes III implemented the redistribution of land after his coup in -227.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch is reflecting on the ethno-political norms of Greek leadership versus Macedonian 'barbarian' hegemony.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch is applying his own 1st/2nd-century CE moral judgment to the 3rd-century BCE political landscape.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The absolute power (autokrator) was a specific legal status granted by the Achaean federal government during emergencies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aratus was given these extraordinary powers to deal with internal dissent and the Cleomenean War in -224.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule specifies the treatment of the private property of tyrants within the city-state of Argos under Achaean influence.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This confiscation and gift to Antigonus Doson occurred after the recovery of Argos in -224.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'law of reprisal' described refers to the specific fate of the people of Mantineia under Achaean law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The sack of Mantineia and the mass enslavement of its population occurred in -223.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch describes the customary division of spoils between allied powers (Achaeans and Macedonians).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This specific 1/3-to-2/3 division of slave proceeds was applied after the fall of Mantineia in -223.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the renaming of this specific city, which was an act of political foundation/re-foundation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Mantineia was renamed Antigoneia in honor of Antigonus Doson in -223.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The scenario concerns the social power dynamics and lack of protections for women at the court of the Macedonian king.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Philip V's corruption of the wife of Aratus the Younger took place around -214 during his stay in Sicyon.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the absolute power of the Antigonid king over his immediate court and subordinates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Philip V executed his former favorites Apelles and Megaleas for sedition in -218.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch describes an 'ancient law' and local superstition specific to the city of Sicyon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The law was discussed and an exception granted upon the death of Aratus in -213.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule details the civic and religious order of a public festival (the Soteria) in the city-state of Sicyon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch notes that 'slight traces' of these rituals were still performed in his own day around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule reflects the legitimacy norms of the Antigonid dynasty in Macedonia regarding royal succession.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This refers to the succession of Perseus to the throne of Macedon upon the death of Philip V in -179.
